original_hypothesis.title: Hook Status Disabling Permanently Locks Rental Assets and Escrowed Payments
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When a rental is created with hooks that have onStop enabled, the hooks are recorded in the order. During stopRent, _removeHooks checks STORE.hookOnStop(target) and reverts if the hook's onStop bit is disabled. If a GUARD_ADMIN disables a hook's onStop status between rental creation and stop, all rentals referencing that hook become permanently un-stoppable. NFTs are locked in the rental Safe and ERC20 payments are locked in escrow indefinitely.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The code makes hook execution mandatory during stop without any bypass or fallback. A legitimate admin operation (disabling a hook) permanently DoSes existing rentals. This is a code architecture defect.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _removeHooks reverts with Shared_DisabledHook if STORE.hookOnStop(target) returns false, with no fallback path

preconditions: Rental created with hooks that have onStop enabled
GUARD_ADMIN disables the hook's onStop bit after rental creation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _removeHooks has no try/catch for disabled hooks and no bypass flag
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Hook status change between rental creation and stop makes stop impossible
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Renter retains NFTs indefinitely; lender loses asset access and escrowed payment permanently

economic_questions: Is there any admin mechanism to re-enable hooks or force-stop rentals?
Can the lender recover escrowed ERC20 tokens through any path?
Could a malicious renter deliberately use hooks then lobby for hook disabling?
validation_requirements: Create rental with hook, disable hook onStop, attempt stopRent and confirm revert
Verify no alternative code path allows stopping without hook execution
Check if admin can re-enable hook to unblock stops

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Zero if opportunistic (wait for admin to disable hook); rental cost if deliberate
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: None - attack is passive (stop simply fails)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of rented NFTs retained by renter indefinitely
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite - renter pays nothing extra to keep assets

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After hook status is changed by admin
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Localized to rentals using the affected hook

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders with active rentals using the disabled hook
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Total loss of rented NFTs + escrowed ERC20 payments
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets in rentals referencing the disabled hook

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Enumerate all active hooks and their associated rental counts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test stopRent with disabled hook to confirm permanent revert

validation_result.mechanism: hook status change blocks rental stop
validation_result.impact_summary: Disabling a hook's onStop bit via updateHookStatus prevents all active rentals referencing that hook from being stopped, locking rented NFTs in rental Safes and ERC20 payments in escrow until the admin re-enables the hook.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis describes a real code defect in the Stop.sol _removeHooks function. When a rental is created with hooks, those hooks are included in the RentalOrder struct and committed into the order hash stored in Storage. During stopRent, the caller must supply the exact RentalOrder (including hooks) whose hash matches the stored hash. The _removeHooks function checks STORE.hookOnStop(target) and reverts with Shared_DisabledHook if the bit is not set. If a GUARD_ADMIN changes the hook's status bitmap between creation and stop (e.g., sets bitmap to 0, 1, 2, or 3—any value without bit 2 set), the hookOnStop check fails and stopRent reverts. The user cannot omit hooks from the order because the hooks are part of the order hash derivation in _deriveRentalOrderHash, and STORE.removeRentals requires orders[orderHash] == true. There is no alternative stop mechanism or emergency bypass. While the admin could theoretically re-enable the hook's onStop status as a workaround, this is an operational band-aid, not a code safeguard. If the hook was disabled because it was found to be buggy or malicious, re-enabling it would re-expose the protocol to the original issue. Furthermore, the try/catch around IHook(target).onStop() only reformats error messages—it still reverts in every catch block, so even the hook call itself failing will block stops.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Stop.sol _removeHooks clearly reverts on disabled hookOnStop status with no fallback. Storage.sol hookOnStop checks bit 2 of the bitmap. Guard.sol updateHookStatus allows GUARD_ADMIN to change the bitmap at any time. The hooks array is cryptographically committed in the order hash via _deriveRentalOrderHash in Signer.sol, preventing users from bypassing hooks during stop.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The hookOnStop check is mandatory with no bypass. If the hook's onStop bit is cleared after rental creation, this check will always fail, causing stopRent to permanently revert for any rental referencing this hook.
Returns false when bit 2 (value 4) of hookStatus is not set, which directly causes _removeHooks to revert.
GUARD_ADMIN can change the bitmap at any time, including clearing the onStop bit, without any check for active rentals using this hook.
Hooks are committed in the order hash, so the caller of stopRent cannot omit or modify hooks to bypass the _removeHooks check. The exact hooks from creation must be supplied.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls Stop.stopRent(order) with the original RentalOrder struct including hooks
5. _removeHooks iterates hooks, calls STORE.hookOnStop(target) for each
6. Storage.hookOnStop checks bit 2 of hookStatus[hook] - returns false if bit cleared
8. Entire stopRent transaction reverts, no state changes occur
9. NFTs remain in rental Safe, ERC20s remain in PaymentEscrow
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: stopRent -> _removeHooks -> STORE.hookOnStop(target) returns false -> revert. The hooks are included in the order hash (Signer._deriveRentalOrderHash includes hookHashes), so the user cannot supply a modified order without hooks. STORE.removeRentals at the end of stopRent requires orders[orderHash] == true, enforcing hash integrity. There is no alternative stop path that bypasses hooks.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployProtocol.s.sol grants GUARD_ADMIN role to deployer, confirming a GUARD_ADMIN exists and can call updateHookStatus. No deployment scripts configure specific hooks, but the mechanism for creating/disabling hooks is clearly present.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) GUARD_ADMIN enables a hook with bitmap=6 (onStart+onStop). (2) Lender creates a rental order with this hook via Seaport. The order is stored with the hook in its struct. (3) GUARD_ADMIN decides to disable the hook (e.g., found to be buggy) and calls updateHookStatus(hook, 0). (4) The rental expires. Anyone calls stopRent with the original RentalOrder. _removeHooks calls STORE.hookOnStop(hook) which returns false (bitmap is 0). Reverts with Shared_DisabledHook. (5) The rented NFTs remain in the rental Safe, and ERC20 payments remain in PaymentEscrow. The renter retains use of the NFTs indefinitely. The only recovery path is for GUARD_ADMIN to re-enable the hook's onStop bit, which may be undesirable if the hook itself is malicious/buggy.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any active rental using hooks whose onStop status is disabled becomes unstoppable. The lender loses access to their NFTs and escrowed payments until the admin re-enables the hook. If the hook was disabled due to being malicious/broken, re-enabling it to unblock stops re-exposes the protocol to the original risk. This is an architectural defect where admin operations can DoS user assets without any safeguard or emergency bypass.
recommended_mitigations: Add a try/catch or conditional skip around the hookOnStop check in _removeHooks, allowing stops to proceed even when a hook is disabled (skip the hook call instead of reverting)
Implement an emergency stop mechanism that bypasses hook execution entirely, callable by an admin or after a grace period
Record hook status at rental creation time and use the recorded status during stop, rather than checking current status
Add a mapping to track which hooks have active rentals, and prevent disabling hooks with active rentals unless an override flag is set
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full protocol with a mock hook contract that has onStart/onStop enabled (bitmap=6). 2. Create a rental order through Seaport with the hook attached. 3. As GUARD_ADMIN, call updateHookStatus(hook, 0) to disable all hook bits. 4. Wait for rental expiry. 5. Call stopRent with the original RentalOrder. 6. Observe revert with Shared_DisabledHook. 7. Verify NFTs still in rental Safe and ERC20s still in escrow.
uncertainties: The admin can re-enable the hook as a workaround, so 'permanently' locked depends on admin responsiveness and willingness to re-enable a potentially problematic hook
The severity depends on whether hooks are commonly used in practice and how frequently hook status changes occur
additional_testing_needed: Test the full scenario end-to-end with a mock hook to confirm the revert behavior
Test whether re-enabling the hook successfully allows the stop to proceed
Test batch stop behavior when only some orders reference the disabled hook
